countries: United States (South Carolina, Florida, Gulf of Mexico)
nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reef slopes, typically well below 30 m and recorded from about 25 to 80 m depth; juveniles have been observed sheltering in floating Sargassum rafts at the surface.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
securely placed rockwork (this species rearranges decor)

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on small crustaceans (crabs, shrimp) and sea urchins. In captivity, generally accepts a varied meaty diet of mysis, krill, chopped shrimp, squid, clams (which also help wear down the continually-growing teeth), and prepared marine carnivore pellets and frozen formulations. Vitamin-enriched and occasional algae-based offerings are also commonly advised.

avoidWith: sessile invertebrates the trigger may sample

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke; 3249x2079. Tack-sharp full-body profile showing the diagnostic yellow-spotted body, blue facial lines, and orange/red-margined caudal fin (the 'red tail'). Best overall.
NURC/UNCW and NOAA/FGBNMS; 2043x1476. Sharp in-situ blue-grey individual with dark spot rows, blue chin lines and orange caudal margins; different colour phase for variety.
Diego Delso; 5342x3561. Striking violet-toned Caribbean (Cozumel) specimen, sharp full-body, showing spotting and orange caudal margins; high-res colour variety.

summary: The Red Tail Triggerfish is one of the calmer members of the triggerfish family, reaching around 20 to 25 cm as an adult. It does best in a spacious fish-only system alongside robust tankmates, with ample open water and secure rockwork to explore.
careNotes:
The Red Tail Triggerfish (Xanthichthys ringens), also widely sold as the Sargassum Triggerfish, is one of the more aquarist-friendly members of the family Balistidae. Adults are generally reported to reach around 20–25 cm. The species is native to the Western Atlantic from the Carolinas and Bermuda through the Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean down to Brazil, typically found on deeper outer-reef slopes at around 25 to 80 m depth.

A tank of around 500 L with a long footprint is a sensible starting point, accounting for this trigger's active swimming and its tendency to rearrange rockwork. Larger systems are advisable for long-term housing or community setups. Provide sturdy rockwork with caves and overhangs, secured so pieces cannot topple when the fish moves them, along with a sand substrate and ample open swimming space.

Water chemistry can follow standard marine parameters: roughly 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, a specific gravity of 1.020–1.026, and carbonate hardness of 8–12 dKH. The species is commonly considered hardy and a generally straightforward keeper once established.

Temperament is commonly described as semi-aggressive but on the calmer end for triggerfish — it has been kept alongside tangs, large angelfishes, wrasses, and other robust tankmates. It can become territorial toward conspecifics in smaller tanks and may sample small ornamental shrimp, crabs, or very small fish, so it is generally regarded as reef-safe only with caution. The species has also been noted to produce audible grunting sounds when stressed or threatened.

Like all triggerfish, the dorsal trigger spine is a structural locking mechanism rather than a venom-delivery system, but the powerful jaws and continually growing teeth can deliver a serious bite. Offering clams or other hard-shelled prey periodically helps wear the teeth down naturally. Lifespan in captivity has not been reliably documented.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Xanthichthys ringens has not been documented in the available aquarium literature. The species is a benthic spawner in the wild but is not regularly bred in captivity given the tank volumes required.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Catalog/product listing for Red Tail Triggerfish. Provided care level (easy), temperament (aggressive — interpreted as semi-aggressive for this species given other sources), reef compatibility (no/with caution), price, and basic care narrative. Listed adult size 'six to ten inches' and minimum tank ~125 gal.

Minimum tank listed as 1500 L — notably higher than the LiveAquaria/community range; flagged as the upper end of the source spread.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable records of captive reproduction in the reviewed aquarium literature. Triggerfish in general are benthic spawners with paired guarding behavior in the wild, but tank-volume and pair-formation challenges have prevented routine captive breeding.
